question:
What is the evaluation criteria for Generative AI and
  LLMs?
answer: The evaluation criteria for Generative AI and LLMs involves a relative grading method, where the final grade is determined by the marks or grades earned during the project's phase-wise evaluations and the end-term assessment. This grading method is outlined in the university's academic regulations, and students must achieve a minimum of 40% of the total marks upon completing all assessments to be eligible for grading. The course includes two programming assignments, each carrying 20% weightage, and a comprehensive project report that carries a 40% weightage, with an expected effort of 20+ hours. The project will be evaluated separately, and all evaluations will be based on the work presented by the students as well as the questions asked, or the problems given to code. The course emphasizes hands-on, application-driven learning, with 80% of the overall course assessment dedicated to experiential learning. This evaluation criteria is supported by the Evaluation Summary and below sections of the Generative AI and LLMs_CourseHandout.pdf.

Course: Generative AI and LLMs
Course Code: CSE3720
Credits: 3 (3-0-0)
Section: Evaluation Summary
Section Type: evaluation

The final grade will be determined by the marks or grades earned during the project’sphase-wise evaluations and the end-term assessment. Grading will be conducted using the relative grading method outlined in the university’sacademic regulations. To be eligible for grading, students must achieve aminimum of 40% of the total marks upon completing all assessments listed in the table below: Evaluation Component Weightage (%) Evaluation Schedule/Timeline Rubrics/Remarks -MCQ knowledge and programming based questions. - Topics to be covered will be CO mapping Quiz 20% April Last Week announced in the class. -20 MCQ questions, each of 1 mark. -Quiz duration 20-30 mins each CO1, CO2 Assignment 1 & 2 2 x 20% = 40% Continuous Assessment Programming Assignments will be given. Offline evaluation to be conducted. Students will be asked to explain the solution with ademo followed by viva. CO1, CO2 Acomprehensive Project report is to End-Sem be submitted along with the coding Evaluation 40% Last week of the Project solutions; this will be followed by course Presentations, Demonstrations & Viva. CO1, CO2, CO3 – All evaluations will be based on the work presented by the students as well as the questions asked, or the problems given to code. – Cases of AI-generated code or plagiarism will be taken seriously and reported according to the university'spolicy on Unfair Means (UFM). It is essential that all work is original and adheres to academic integrity. – Experiential Learning Component (80%): The course includes two programming assignments, each carrying 20% weightage (total 40%), aimed at developing practical skills in implementing and applying Generative AI and LLM concepts. In addition, the project component also serves as one of the experiential learning activities for this course. Students will work on real-world problem statements requiring the design and implementation of Generative AI and LLM-based solutions, such as prompt engineering, retrieval-augmented generation, model adaptation, or evaluation of generative outputs. Students are expected to implement the solution, analyze results, and assess model behaviour with respect to quality and ethical considerations. The project carries a 40% weightage, with an expected effort of 20+ hours, and will be evaluated separately. Collectively, these components emphasize hands-on, application-driven learning, with 80% of the overall course assessment dedicated to experiential learning.

Course: Generative AI and LLMs
Course Code: CSE3720
Credits: 3 (3-0-0)
Section: following
Section Type: general

Course Outcome Statement Mapped Component A ssociated Activities / Assessment CO1 Apply core Large Language Model (LLM) concepts and effective prompting Employability  Interactive theory sessions on LLM basics and prompting techniques to solve real world problems CO2 Analyze the Generative AI lifecycle and model architectures to determine their suitability for different application domains. Skill Development  In-class tool demonstrations (ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini)  Industry use-case studies and discussion  Comparative evaluation of models such as VAEs, GANs & Transformer  Implementation walkthroughs using Python libraries (TensorFlow/Keras/ PyTorch)  Mini assignments: Choose the best architecture for a use case & justify why. CO3 Evaluate ethical and responsible AI frameworks and apply them in the design and implementation of safe, accountable, and effective Generative AI solutions. Entrepreneurship + Skill Development  Case-studies on AI ethics, hallucinations, fairness issue  Building small working prototypes using LangChain, RAG, LoRA, etc.  Getting familiar with cloud services (HuggingFace Hub, Blynk/Gradio demo, OpenAI endpoints, etc.)  Capstone/mini-project: Build & demonstrate an LLM-powered solution relevant to industry or startup use Prerequisite: if any 1. Python programming skills 2. Fundamentals of Machine Learning Topics of the course: Topics Number of Sessions (48) Course Outcomes Unit 1: Generative AI Overview: What is generative AI, Different types of generative AI models, Understand the principles and applications of Generative AI in creating new data instances, Understand the principles behind Generative AI: Get familiar with building and tweaking generative models for any real-world use case. Generative AI Project Lifecycle. Unit 2: Generative AI Models: Seq2Seq Models and Variational Autoencoders (VAEs): Sequence-to-Sequence models, Variational Autoencoders, Text-to-Text generation, Image and text-based VAE applications. Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs): Understanding GAN architecture, GAN training process, GAN applications in image and video generation. 2 sessions 2 Theory 6 sessions 2 Theory + 4 Lab CO1 CO1 Unit 3: Transformer: Learn about the architecture of Transformer models, including attention mechanisms, encoders, and decoders, Pre-training and fine-tuning strategies. Gain deeper insights into the capabilities and potential of Transformer technology. 6 sessions 2 Theory + 4 Lab CO1, CO2 Unit 4: Large Language Models: Learn about Language Models (LLMs) and their role in understanding and generating human-like text, Different types of LLMs: Small Language Models (SLMs), Dense Language Models, etc. Different training datasets for LLMs. Training methods for LLMs, Challenges in training LLMs, Different evaluation metrics for LLMs, Challenges in evaluating LLMs, Practical Implementation of LLMs, and how fine-tuning enables LLMs to be adapted to a variety of specific use cases. Data privacy and local LLMs. Introduction to LangChain for building LLM applications. Unit 5: Prompts Module: Understanding the concept and significance of prompt engineering, strategies for designing effective prompts, Techniques for controlling model behaviour and output quality. Prompting Techniques: Reasoning and Acting (ReAct), Chain-of-Thought (CoT). Unit 6: LLM Fine-Tuning Techniques Fine-tuning and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT), Low Rank Adaptation (LoRA), Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF). Unit 7: Retrieval-Augmented & Tool-Enhanced LLM Workflows Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) – vector database and similarity search, Implementing RAG with LangChain. Overview of recently

Student Responsibilities: – Attend lectures and do the work Lab Assignments as per instructions. – Participate in the discussions/assignments held during classes. – Check announcements on the LMS and emails regularly. – Submit the assigned task on time. – Regularly check marks on the LMS to ensure they are up to date. – Participate in class and take necessary actions to grasp the material. Asking questions is encouraged. – Communicate any concerns by speaking directly with the instructor. A ttendance Policy: Students are expected to attend classes regularly. Failure to follow the classes regularly and adhere to the expected attendance percentage will result in losing quiz/lab marks and a reduction of the grade as per the University’s grading policy. Recourse Examination Policy: This is a practical course with a lot of hands-on activities. There will be no recourse examination. Students failing in the course must repeat the course when it is offered next. Make-up policy: No make-up exam will be conducted for unexcused absences.
